responses: We built an AI-powered clinical documentation assistant for a network of outpatient specialty clinics. The system listens to physician-patient encounters in real time, generates structured clinical notes in the clinic's EHR-compatible format, and flags documentation gaps — missing diagnoses, unsigned orders, incomplete medication reconciliation — before the physician exits the encounter. The problem it solves is that physicians at this clinic spent an average of 2.1 hours per day on documentation after clinic hours, which was identified as the leading driver of burnout in the practice's annual survey. Post-deployment measurement over 9 months shows after-hours documentation time reduced to 38 minutes per day, with no statistically significant change in note quality as measured by chart audit scores, and a 14-point improvement in physician satisfaction scores.

We evaluated four architectural approaches: a pure transcription pipeline feeding a downstream summarization model; a structured dictation system requiring physicians to follow a template during the encounter; a specialty-trained clinical NLP model; and the ambient AI approach using a general-purpose model with a structured clinical output schema. We rejected structured dictation because prior implementation attempts in 2019 showed physician compliance dropped to 40% within 3 months — imposing structure on the encounter behavior was not sustainable. The clinical NLP model was trained on general clinical text and performed poorly on the specialty-specific terminology and phrasing patterns of this practice. The transcription-plus-summarization pipeline introduced unacceptable latency for real-time flagging. We chose the ambient approach because it imposed no workflow change on the physician, produced notes in the practice's established format, and met the 500ms flagging latency requirement. The explicit tradeoffs we accepted: higher inference cost per encounter, dependency on a third-party ambient listening hardware component with its own reliability characteristics, and the risk that physicians would over-rely on the system and reduce their own internal documentation discipline.

We identified five failure modes and rated them by likelihood and impact. The highest-severity failure is a clinical error in the generated note that goes unsigned and becomes part of the medical record — medication name confusion and dosage transcription errors are the specific patterns we tested against. Mitigation: all generated notes require physician review and signature before EHR write; the system presents diffs versus the physician's prior note for the same patient to highlight changes. Second: ambient audio misidentification — capturing conversation from an adjacent exam room and attributing it to the wrong patient. Mitigation: hardware zones are isolated by room; the system uses speaker diarization to flag uncertainty when more than two speakers are detected. Third: model degradation on rare specialties within the practice (we have two sub-specialties with fewer than 300 encounters in the training set). Mitigation: specialty-specific accuracy monitoring with a monthly manual audit of 20 charts per subspecialty. We have not yet implemented automated monitoring for this mode. Fourth: the physician review step becomes rubber-stamping — physicians approve notes without reading them. Mitigation: we track review dwell time and flag physicians whose average dwell time falls below 30 seconds. Fifth: PHI leakage through the third-party audio hardware. Mitigation: BAA in place with the hardware vendor; audio is processed on-device and only the transcript is sent to the cloud.

The most important learning from this build was that our initial evaluation framework measured note completeness but not note accuracy — we were counting whether the required fields were populated, not whether the values were correct. This masked a 4% error rate on medication dosages in the pilot cohort that was not caught until a physician flagged an anomaly during chart review in week 3. We immediately added accuracy spot checks to our evaluation protocol and rebuilt our monitoring dashboard around accuracy metrics rather than completeness metrics. The model had been confidently producing dosage values that were plausible but wrong — the values it generated were within normal ranges, which is why they passed our completeness-only checks. This incident fundamentally changed how we approach evaluation for any AI system that produces medical content: we now require an accuracy evaluation with ground truth annotation before any clinical deployment.

rationale: Uniform L4 profile. Comprehensive across all eight dimensions. Multiple measurable outcomes, explicit exclusions, four alternatives with named tradeoffs, five failure modes with mitigations and containment strategies, and reflection that directly changed evaluation methodology. Override evidence earns 4 because a pattern of model errors was identified with a systematic response, though not yet a documented framework for when to accept vs override.
